summary: High‑energy MIT set with blistering Dancin' In The Street and soaring Morning Dew
blurb: The show bursts open with a ferocious 'Dancin' In The Street' followed by a seamless China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider transition. Highlights include a powerful Good Lovin'/St. Stephen/Not Fade Away medley and an emotional 'Morning Dew'. Jerry’s guitar is on fire while Phil drives the low end, making this a must‑listen early‑70 performance.
review: May 6, 1970 at Kresge Plaza captured one of the Grateful Dead’s most incendiary moments. Fresh off the Kent State tragedy, the band launched into an angry, high‑octane set that never let up – from the blistering opening ‘Dancin' In The Street’ to the soaring, tear‑filled ‘Morning Dew’. The China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider segue flows like a river of fire, and the Good Lovin'/St. Stephen/Not Fade Away sequence builds into a jaw‑dropping climax before resolving into the heartfelt farewell of ‘Morning Dew’. Jerry’s SG was thrashing and gritty, delivering solos described as “monster” and “self‑generating electricity,” while Phil’s bass ran with devastating authority, especially on the opening number. Bob’s vocals hold steady despite a few mic quirks, and Bill Kreutzmann provides a soundscape of percussion that underpins every jam. The recording quality varies – the recommended GANS‑Hall SBD is raw but clear enough to feel the room’s urgency, making this show a vivid snapshot of the Dead’s early‑70 intensity.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All five recordings are marked positive, repeatedly praising the same standout songs (Dancin', Good Lovin', Morning Dew) and Jerry’s ferocious guitar tone, Phil’s driving bass, and Bill’s percussion. Multiple sources call the performance ‘incendiary’, ‘monster’, and ‘tight’, indicating strong consensus of exceptional playing.

best_recording.reason: Good soundboard capture with clear, sharp mix; retains the raw energy of the night while keeping the full set intact despite occasional distortion.

key_highlights: Blistering opening ‘Dancin' In The Street’ that sets an angry, high‑energy tone
Seamless China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider transition
Epic Good Lovin'/St. Stephen/Not Fade Away medley building to a powerful climax
Emotional, soaring ‘Morning Dew’ finale that captures the political urgency of the era

band_performance.Jerry: Thrashing his SG with a ferocious, gritty tone; solos described as ‘monster’, ‘self‑generating electricity’ and thundering yet delicate, keeping the set on fire.
band_performance.Phil: Devastating bass runs drive the improvisations; lead‑bass work highlighted on ‘Dancin' In The Street’, ‘Morning Dew’, and throughout the set with authoritative low‑end presence.
band_performance.Bob: Solid vocal delivery despite occasional mic issues; adds rhythmic guitar support and shares vocal duties, including a spoken announcement at show’s end.
band_performance.Billy: Bill Kreutzmann’s drumming forms a ‘soundscape’, providing great percussion, tight intro on ‘Good Lovin'’ and steady warm backing all night.
